steps.title: Match evidence to the claim
steps.content: Gayah claims that eliminating left turns at busy urban intersections would improve traffic flow and reduce overall travel times, even if some drivers must travel slightly farther. Strong support should show better travel efficiency after left turns were eliminated, not just easier navigation or a comparison between different signal types.

steps.title: Identify the supporting outcome
steps.content: The package-delivery finding describes a city after left turns were eliminated at busy intersections. The company reports that its drivers reached more addresses each day and delivered more packages annually. Reaching more destinations per day suggests that travel between stops became faster overall, which is the practical effect Gayah predicts.

steps.title: Reject wrong interventions
steps.content: Choice A describes adding left-turn signals, not eliminating turns. Choice B compares intersections with and without left-turn signals while left turns still exist, so it does not test Gayah's restriction. Choice D says school buses took longer after left turns were eliminated at most intersections, which weakens the claim instead of supporting it. The answer is C.
